coredata.dc:title: Molecularly imprinted polymers-isolated AuNP-enhanced CdTe QD fluorescence sensor for selective and sensitive oxytetracycline detection in real water samples
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Yuanyuan
Liu, Xiaohui
Meng, Shuang
Mao, Shun
Tao, Wenquan
Li, Zhuo
coredata.dc:description: A molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs)-isolated AuNP-enhanced fluorescence sensor, AuNP@MIPs-CdTe QDs, was developed for highly sensitive and selective detection of oxytetracycline (OTC) in aqueous medium. The developed sensor combined the advantages of strong fluorescence signal of metal-enhanced fluorescence (MEF), high selectivity of MIPs, and stability of CdTe QDs. The MIPs shell with specific recognition served as an isolation layer to adjust the distance between AuNP and CdTe QDs to optimize the MEF system. The sensor demonstrated the detection limit as low as 5.22 nM (2.40 μg/L) for a concentration range of 0.1–3.0 μM OTC and good recovery rates of 96.0–103.0% in real water samples. In addition, high specificity recognition for OTC over its analogs was achieved with an imprinting factor of 6.10. Molecular dynamics (MD) simulation was utilized to simulate the polymerization process of MIPs and revealed H-bond formation as the mainly binding sites of APTES and OTC, and finite-difference time-domain (FDTD) analysis was employed to obtain the distribution of electromagnetic field (EM) for AuNP@MIPs-CdTe QDs. The experimental results combined with theoretical analyses not only provided a novel MIP-isolated MEF sensor with excellent detection performance for OTC but also established a theoretical basis for the development of a new generation of sensors.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) The fabrication procedure of AuNP@MIPs-CdTe QDs sensor. (B) The recognition process for OTC by AuNP@MIPs-CdTe QDs sensor. (C) Enhancement mechanism of metal-enhanced fluorescence of AuNP@MIPs-CdTe QDs sensor.

Typical TEM images of (A) AuNP, (B) CdTe QDs, and (C) AuNP@MIPs-CdTe QDs. Typical TEM images of AuNP@MIPs-CdTe QDs with the MIPs thickness of (D) 3.1 ± 0.4 nm, (E) 8.5 ± 0.9 nm, and (F)20.7 ± 1.2 nm respectively. (G) Shell thickness distribution of AuNP@MIPs-CdTe QDs with the different amounts of TOES. (H) Absorption spectra of AuNP solution and AuNP@MIPs-CdTe QDs with different MIPs thickness.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of CdTe QDs (green line), AuNP@NIPs-CdTe QDs (black line), AuNP@MIPs-CdTe QDs (red line), and AuNP@MIPs-CdTe QDs without eluted (blue line). (B) Fluorescence emission spectra and photograph (inset, under ultraviolet lamps) of CdTe QDs and AuNP@MIPs-CdTe QDs with different MIPs shell thickness. (C) FDTD simulation of the EM field enhancement around AuNP. (D) The time-resolved PL decay spectra and fitting curves of AuNP@MIPs-CdTe QDs the in the absence and presence of OTC. (E) Schematic for the fluorescence quenching mechanism of electron transfer from AuNP@MIPs-CdTe QDs to OTC.

(A) Snapshots of the initial simulation box and the same box after conducting the MD simulations (The template molecules OTC are shown as red, functional monomer APTES as blue, crosslinking agent TEOS as green, and water as wathet blue). (B) Optimized geometries of APTES and OTC molecules. (C) RDFs, g(r), between specific sites of O1-O9 of OTC molecules and N of APTES molecules.

Fluorescence spectra and contour plots (insets) of (A) AuNP@MIPs-CdTe QDs sensor. (B) AuNP@NIPs-CdTe QDs sensor for detection of OTC with concentrations 0–3.0 μM. Stern-Volmer plots for (C)AuNP@MIPs-CdTe QDs and (D)AuNP@NIPs-CdTe QDs sensors with OTC concentration of 0.05–3.0 μM, respectively.

(A) Fluorescence quenching efficiencies of AuNP@MIPs-CdTe QDs and AuNP@NIPs-CdTe QDs sensors in the presence of 1 μM OTC, TC, DOX, ENR, and FF, respectively. (Inset: chemical structures of analogs). (B) The RSD analysis results from the fluorescence quenching efficiencies of AuNP@MIPs-CdTe QDs sensor.
Spiked recoveries with RSDs (%, n = 3) for the detection of OTC in tap water, river water, and lake water samples using AuNP@MIPs-CdTe QDs sensor.
Comparison of AuNP@MIPs-CdTe QDs sensor with other FL sensors or SMI-Fl sensors for detection of OTC.

head.ce:title: Molecularly imprinted polymers-isolated AuNP-enhanced CdTe QD fluorescence sensor for selective and sensitive oxytetracycline detection in real water samples

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs)-isolated AuNP-enhanced fluorescence sensor, AuNP@MIPs-CdTe QDs, was developed for highly sensitive and selective detection of oxytetracycline (OTC) in aqueous medium. The developed sensor combined the advantages of strong fluorescence signal of metal-enhanced fluorescence (MEF), high selectivity of MIPs, and stability of CdTe QDs. The MIPs shell with specific recognition served as an isolation layer to adjust the distance between AuNP and CdTe QDs to optimize the MEF system. The sensor demonstrated the detection limit as low as 5.22 nM (2.40 μg/L) for a concentration range of 0.1–3.0 μM OTC and good recovery rates of 96.0–103.0% in real water samples. In addition, high specificity recognition for OTC over its analogs was achieved with an imprinting factor of 6.10. Molecular dynamics (MD) simulation was utilized to simulate the polymerization process of MIPs and revealed H-bond formation as the mainly binding sites of APTES and OTC, and finite-difference time-domain (FDTD) analysis was employed to obtain the distribution of electromagnetic field (EM) for AuNP@MIPs-CdTe QDs. The experimental results combined with theoretical analyses not only provided a novel MIP-isolated MEF sensor with excellent detection performance for OTC but also established a theoretical basis for the development of a new generation of sensors.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Oxytetracycline (OTC) is a member of tetracycline (TC) antibiotics commonly used for rapid treatments of bacterial infectious diseases in humans, animal husbandry, and aquaculture [1,2]. However, OTC is primarily excreted in its original form via feces and urine after ingestion and absorption owing to its incomplete degradation [3], leading to the long-term presence of antibiotics in the water environment. This not only has adverse impacts on the whole ecosystem but harms to human health through transmission in the food chain [4]. The World Health Organization (WHO) stipulates that the provisional maximum daily intake of OTC is 0.15 μg/g, while the maximum limit of residue is 0.1 µg/g [5]. In China, the recommended value of OTC for freshwater organisms in surface water environmental quality standards is 18.7 μg/L based on a comprehensive evaluation [6]. Hence, to develop a highly effective approach for detecting OTC in the water environment is imperative.

Conventional methods for detecting trace OTC include thin-layer chromatography [7], high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [8], and liquid chromatography tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS-MS) [9]. Nevertheless, owing to the intricacy of the matrices and the low concentration of OTC, those methods typically necessitate time-consuming sample preparation and separation operations. In the last decade, various convenient, fast, and sensitive analytical methods involving colorimetric [10], fluorescence [11,12], electrochemical [13] and surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) [14] have been developed for detecting antibiotics. Whereas fluorescence analysis stands out among these methods due to its straightforward operation, excellent sensitivity, and economical instrumentation. Li et al. designed novel clusteroluminogens by modifying cyclodextrin with amino acids, which facilitated intermolecular and intramolecular clusterization of chromophores within the confined space of cyclodextrin [12]. This method has been proven to be applicable for fluorescence detection of chlortetracycline with high sensitivity and selectivity. Additionally, increasing fluorescence emission intensity is always crucial for a fluorescence sensor to detect the target molecules in trace level [15].

To achieve stronger fluorescence intensity, metal-enhanced fluorescence (MEF) was proposed, where fluorescence enhancement occurred when a fluorophore was localized near the surface of metallic nanostructures such as nanoparticles, nanorods, nanocubes, and so on (primarily Ag, Au, and Cu) [16-18]. The enhancement of fluorescence intensity was attributed to the localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) excitation in the metal, which generated the local field enhancement, resulting in increased absorption and emission cross sections of the sample. The effects of fluorescence enhancement were strongly dependent on structural and optical factors in metallic nanostructures and fluorophores [19]. Specifically, one of the factors is the distance between metallic nanostructures and fluorophores. The fluorescence is competitively quenched when fluorophores are in close proximity to the metal nanostructures. On the contrary, by optimizing the distance to fall within the range of 5–30 nm, the significantly enhanced efficiency of excitation can be observed [20]. Therefore, the MEF effect can be optimized by adjusting the distance between metallic nanostructures and fluorophores to improve the fluorescence intensity [21]. Another factor is the spectral overlap between fluorophore emission and metallic nanostructures absorption [22]. Notably, the resonance of metal nanostructures can interact with the frequencies emitted by the fluorophore, resulting in the metal emitting light at the same frequency (elastic scattering) as the luminescence emitted by the fluorophores. This phenomenon consequently leads to a significantly enhancement in the intensity of the emitted light [22]. Wang et al. reported a DNA aptamer-labeled silica-coated Ag nanoparticles-enhanced fluorescence spectroscopy (AgNPs@SiO2/QDs-Apt) for detecting TC [15]. The Ag@SiO2/QD with the SiO2 thickness of 9 nm exhibited strong fluorescence emission intensity, which was 3.9-fold higher than that without a metal core. However, traditional MEF nanoprobes in complex environments were easily disrupted by the presence of interferences with similar structures. Therefore, it is necessary to improve the higher specific selectivity of MEF nanoprobe.

In order to improve the selective enrichment and discrimination of fluorophores, molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) have been proposed as promising recognition materials [23]. MIPs exhibit excellent performance in specific molecular recognition capabilities [24]. Specifically, MIPs are synthesized in the presence of a template for the target analyte and a functional monomer that forms a polymeric complex by cross-linking [25]. After complete polymerization, the template is eluted to obtain recognition cavities which are specific to the target analyte [26]. The combination of optical sensors with MIPs not only exhibits good optical and signal detection performance but also demonstrates high selectivity and stability [27–30]. We have previously reported a polydopamine-based MIP-SERS sensor for the detection of dimethyl phthalate, which exhibited low SERS interference for structural analogs [31].

The traditional MIPs have limited applications due to low selectivity and adsorption capacity [32]. Surface molecularly imprinted polymers (SMIPs), as a branch of MIPs, have been vigorously developed in recent years [33]. As templated targets are mostly located near or on the surface of nanostructures, the template molecules can be almost completely removed, reducing the detection background and leading to faster binding dynamics and binding ability, making the recognition sites achieve better anti-interference ability [34]. Based on the above advantages, SMIPs combined with fluorescence detection technology, known as SMIPs-based fluorescence sensors (SMI-Fl sensors) have been widely used [35–37]. However, the concept of combining MEF with SMI-Fl sensors for OTC detection has not been extensively explored yet but could have an important influence in practical applications.

Herein, we developed a novel approach to detect OTC in water samples by combining the advantages of a strong fluorescence signal of MEF, high selectivity of SMIPs, and stability of CdTe QDs. MIPs-isolated AuNP-enhanced fluorescence sensor named as AuNP@MIPs-CdTe QDs was prepared via decorating MIPs shell onto AuNP using OTC as template molecules, 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES) as functional monomers, and tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS) as cross-linker agents. CdTe QDs were synthesized by a simple hydrothermal method and employed as fluorescent signal probes to be fixed on AuNP@MIPs. After OTC removal by elution, AuNP@MIPs-CdTe QDs exhibited specific recognition ability towards the corresponding template. Adjusting the thickness of the MIP shell, that was, the amount or ratio of functional monomers, template molecules, and crosslinking agents, the effect of the MIPs shell on enhanced fluorescence performance was studied. The prepared AuNP@MIPs-CdTe QDs sensor for the detection of OTC was investigated and characterized thoroughly, and its recognition properties were well-demonstrated. Furthermore, molecular dynamics (MD) simulations were performed to investigate the interactions between template molecules and functional monomers, and finite-difference time-domain (FDTD) simulation was conducted to reveal the underlying mechanisms for MEF. The developed MIPs-isolated AuNP-enhanced fluorescence sensor demonstrated excellent selectivity for the detection of trace OTC in real water samples, making it a promising candidate for OTC detection.

Tellurium powder (99%), CdCl2·5/2 H2O, tetraethoxysilane (TEOS), hydrogen tetrachloroaurate (III) trihydrate (HAuCl4·3 H2O, >99.0%), and HCl were purchased from Adamas Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Ammonia solution (NH3·H2O), 3-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), (3-aminopropyl) triethoxysilane (APTES), ethanol, 3-mercaptopropionic acid (MPA), and were supplied by Aladdin Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). OTC, NaOH, NaBH4, and ascorbic acid were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). All chemicals utilized in this study were of at least analytical purity grade and used without further purification unless otherwise stated. All aqueous solutions were prepared with deionized water obtained from a Merck Millipore laboratory water system (Direct-Q3 UV, Tondino, China) with a specific resistance of 18.2 MΩ.

The morphologies and the chemical composition of the AuNP@MIPs-CdTe QDs were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (S-4800, Hitachi, Japan). The crystalline structure characterization was conducted by X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectra on an X-ray powder diffractometer (D/max-2600PC, Rigaku, Japan) with CuKα radiation in the 2θ range of 5°− 80°. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrophotometry spectra (4000–400 cm−1) were obtained from FT-IR spectrophotometer (Nicolet IS 20, Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). The transient fluorescence lifetimes were measured using steady state and transient state fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh FLS1000, UK). The UV–vis spectra of various materials and the process of template removal were measured using a UV–vis spectrophotometer (DR 6000, HACH Instruments, China).

AuNPs with a diameter of ∼50 nm was synthesized following the detailed procedure provided in SI S1. The AuNP@MIPs was synthesized using APTES as the functional monomers, OTC as the template molecule, and TEOS as the cross-linker using a sol-gel copolymerization process, as reported in previous work [38]. The pH of the AuNPs solution was adjusted to 10.5 with 0.1 M NaOH. Followed by the addition of 30 μL of 20% TEOS (in ethanol), the mixture was gently stirred for 30 min. Subsequently, a mixture of 10 mg of OTC and 42 μL of APTES was stirred for 30 min. Then, 100, 200, and 300 μL of 20% TEOS (in ethanol) were added under vigorous stirring for a further 30 min, respectively. The mixture solution was aged for 20 h to allow the formation of MIPs shells. A non-molecularly imprinted polymers (NIPs)-coated AuNPs (AuNP@NIPs) was synthesized in the same preparation method but without the addition of the OTC template.

AuNP@MIPs-CdTe QDs sensor was prepared by functionalizing the AuNP@MIPs with amino groups and carboxylated water-soluble CdTe QDs (SI S2) were modified onto the amino-functionalized AuNP@MIPs surface using cross-linking agent EDC [39]. Briefly, a mixture of 20 μL of APTES, 10 mL of anhydrous ethanol solution, and obtained AuNP@MIPs was stirred at 25 ℃ for 2 h and then heated at 50 ℃ for 1 h. The carboxyl group of MPA-CdTe QDs was activated by adding 10 mL of CdTe QDs solution under an ice bath condition. Then, 0.5 mL of 10 mM EDC solution was added, and the solution was stirred for 15 min. Afterward, 5 mL of APTES-AuNPs@MIPs solution was added, and the mixed suspension was stirred at 0 ℃ for 2 h, followed by continuing the reaction at 25 ℃ for 4 h. To remove the templates OTC, the prepared AuNP@MIPs-CdTe QDs was ultrasound and washed with eluent (V ethanol/V concentrated ammonia/V water= 7:2:1) until no trace of templates were detected via UV–vis spectroscopy. Similarly, the CdTe QDs-decorated AuNP@NIPs sensor (AuNP@NIPs-CdTe QDs sensor) was prepared in the same manner.

The analyzed samples were mixed with AuNP@MIPs-CdTe QD sensor at a ratio of 1:1 and then shaken, to obtain a final concentration of 0–3 μM OTC in PBS (pH=7.0). The fluorescence measurements were recorded on a spectrofluorometer (F-320, Guangdong Sci.&Tech., China). The fluorescence spectra were conducted using the excitation wavelength of 335 nm and recording the emission range of 400–650 nm. A similar process of fluorescence measurements was used for AuNP@NIPs-CdTe QDs sensor to detect OTC.

To evaluate the practicality of the developed sensor for detecting OTC, the real samples were collected from the laboratory, the Huangpu River (Shanghai, China), and the Dianshan Lake (Shanghai, China). The collected samples were subjected to centrifugation at 5000 rpm for 5 min to remove large particles and then filtered through a 0.45 µm microfiltration membrane to filter the suspended matter prior to analysis. OTC solutions were added into each sample to achieve the final concentrations of 0.1 μM, 0.5 μM, 1 μM and 1.5 μM for analysis.

MD studies were conducted to deeply investigate the interaction mechanisms between the template molecules and the functional monomers [40]. The simulation box was set up with 10 OTC molecules, 80 APTES molecules, and 2000 water molecules to mimic the experimental conditions. The optimized potentials for liquid simulation all-atom (OPLS-AA) force field were adopted to model the compound molecules [41], while the SPC/E model was employed for water molecules [42]. The initial simulation box was generated using the Moltemplate package [43] and LigParGen web server [44–46].

All MD simulations were conducted with the Large-scale Atomic/Molecular Massively Parallel Simulator (LAMMPS) package [47,48]. In these simulations, periodic boundary conditions were implemented, and the simulation box dimensions were approximately 4.5 × 4.5 × 4.5 nm3. The simulation methodology included the following steps: (i) the system underwent energy minimization for 10 ps at 0 K to avoid any potential atom-atom close contacts; (ii) relaxation in isothermal-isobaric ensemble (NPT) at 298 K and 1 bar for 500 ps; (iii) annealing for 1 ns in canonical ensemble (NVT) at 370 K; (iv) equilibration at 298 K for 2 ns to achieve stable values in energy, pressure, and density; (v) production run for 5 ns in NVT ensemble and the data during the last 2 ns were collected for calculations and analyses.

The finite-difference time-domain (FDTD) calculation with Lumerical FDTD Solutions software was performed to obtain the EM field distribution of AuNP@MIPs-CdTe QDs to reveal the underlying mechanisms for MEF [49]. The diameter of AuNP and the MIPs shell was considered by the TEM images. In the simulation, a mesh size of 0.5 nm was employed to improve the precision of the results, and the AuNP@MIPs-CdTe QDs were surrounded by water (n = 1.33). The nanostructure was excited with broadband light from the top in the +X direction, while perfectly matched layer boundaries were used in the XYZ direction. The total-field scattered-field monitor was positioned in proximity to the nanostructure to measure the near-field properties. The near-field enhancement was determined by computing the integral volume average of |E/E 0|4 by the following Eq.(1) [50].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication and characterization of AuNP@MIPs-CdTe QDs sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The fabrication procedure of AuNP@MIPs-CdTe QDs is illustrated in Fig. 1 A, in which AuNPs were selected as the core support and metal-enhanced materials, and the MIPs shell was utilized to control the distance between the AuNP core and CdTe QDs. Specifically, AuNPs were synthesized followed by the seed-mediated growth method. Subsequently, a MIPs shell was synthesized on the surface of AuNPs using a sol-gel copolymerization process. The carboxylated-CdTe QDs were then conjugated with amine-terminated AuNP@MIPs using an EDC coupling agent. Finally, the template molecules were removed, resulting in the formation of cavities with specific binding sites for the target analytes on the as-synthesized AuNP@MIPs-CdTe QDs sensor. Fig. 1 B schematically shows the recognition process for the detection of OTC by the prepared AuNP@MIPs-CdTe QDs sensor. The interaction was established between APTES and OTC which would be broken by eluent, creating imprinted cavities with specific recognition sites. The imprinted cavities could match the size and shape of OTC, forming highly specific binding sites with recognition for OTC. The MIPs shell could not only enhance the selectivity via the specific recognition of MIPs cavities but also regulate the spacing between AuNP and CdTe QDs to obtain the optimal distance for metal-enhanced fluorescence. As shown in Fig. 1 C, AuNP@MIPs-CdTe QDs, where the AuNP@MIPs acted as an LSPR generating nanoantenna to enhanced EM fields at the CdTe QDs surface, thereby improving light absorption. Excitation by incident light can trigger the generation of EM fields through LSPR, which can significantly hasten the radiative decay of excited states in CdTe QDs, resulting in enhanced spontaneous emission rate by modulating the local optical density of states (LODS) around the emitter [51]. Moreover, the MIPs shell can protect the AuNP from direct contact with CdTe QDs, thereby reducing quenching effects.

The TEM images of bare AuNP, CdTe QDs, and AuNPs@MIPs-CdTe QDs are shown in Fig. 2 A-C, respectively. AuNPs, CdTe QDs, and AuNPs@MIPs-CdTe QDs show clear profiles with a diameter of 40.2 ± 2.4 nm, 2.8 ± 0.3, and 48.6 ± 1.2 nm, respectively ( Fig. S1 ). Following the process of polymerization, a uniform layer of MIPs shell was modified on the surface of AuNP, with the thickness controlled by adjusting the amount of TEOS. Fig. 2 D-F illustrates that the MIPs thickness on the AuNP surface was 3.1 ± 0.4 nm, 8.5 ± 0.9 nm, and 20.7 ± 1.2 nm, for 100, 200, and 300 μL of 20% TEOS (in ethanol), respectively. The corresponding shell thickness distribution of AuNP@MIPs-CdTe QDs is presented in Fig. 2 G. As revealed by UV–vis absorption spectra in Fig. 2 H, the LSPR peak of AuNP@MIPs showed an increased redshift as the thickness of the MIPs layer increased. After MIPs modification, the previous ambient medium of water (refractive index 1.33) surrounding AuNP was replaced by MIPs with a higher refractive index of 1.46, resulting in the observed redshift of the AuNP absorption peak. The formation was confirmed by XRD patterns (Fig. S2), which shows a crystalline П-stacked structure of MIPs at a 2θ value of around 23.5° and four distinct peaks of (111), (200), (220), and (311) crystal planes of AuNP. These results further indicated that the AuNP@MIPs-CdTe QDs was prepared successfully. The FT-IR spectra of AuNP@SiO2, AuNP@NIPs, AuNP@MIPs, and AuNP@MIPs-CdTe QDs are shown in Fig. S3. The absorption peaks at 468 and 798 cm−1 were the bending vibration peak and symmetric stretching vibration peak of Si-O, respectively, and the stretching vibration peak of Si-O-Si was at 1097 cm−1 [52]. A broad adsorption band at 3442 cm−1 was attributed to the O-H stretching vibration and N-H stretching vibration. Theoretically, the aliphatic C-H stretching band typically occurred around 2928 cm−1. However, due to the low sensitivity of FT-IR spectrophotometry and the relatively less number of C-H bonds, it appeared as a very weak peak in the spectra [53]. Compared with AuNP@SiO2, the peaks at 1558 cm−1 appeared in other substances, which were attributed to the N-H band flexural vibration of APTES, indicating successful modification of MIPs and NIPs layers on the surface of AuNP [38]. The peak intensity of MIPs layer was significantly higher than that of NIPs, and a redshift phenomenon occurred, which was attributed to the formation of hydrogen bonding (N-H--O) between OTC and APTES. Following the modification of CdTe QDs, an absorption peak appeared at 1219 cm−1, which was attributed to the C-N stretching vibration with the formation of the amide bond formed between the amino group of AuNP@MIPs and the carboxyl groups of CdTe QDs. Those observations confirmed the successful composite formation of AuNP@MIPs-CdTe QDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Condition optimization on the fluorescence properties of AuNP@MIPs-CdTe QDs
ce:section.ce:para: The emission spectrum and excitation spectrum of CdTe QDs upon excitation were closely related to the duration of heating and reflux time during the preparation process. Fig. S4 shows the emission spectra of CdTe QDs during the reflux time extended from 1 h to 8 h, indicating a significant shift in the maximum emission wavelength towards the longer wavelength side. The fluorescence color with 365 nm ultraviolet light gradually changed from green to orange and then to red, while the color had almost no change in sunlight (Fig. S5). As shown in Fig. S6, when the SPR absorption peak of AuNP@MIPs (λabsorption=532 nm) overlaps with the fluorescence emission peak of green-CdTe QDs (λemission=527 nm), the metal in AuNPs could emit light through elastic scattering at the same frequency as CdTe QDs, thereby enhancing fluorescence signal intensities. Thus, the green-CdTe QDs were selected as the fluorescence signal source in this work.

As shown in Fig. 3 A, CdTe QDs, AuNP@MIPs-CdTe QDs, and AuNP@NIPs-CdTe QDs exhibited similar emission spectra, with the maximum peaks centered around 530 nm, which revealed CdTe QDs were the sole source of fluorescence in the MEF system. The emission spectra of AuNP@MIPs-CdTe QDs and AuNP@NIPs-CdTe QDs (λemission=532 nm) showed a slight red shift in comparison with that of the CdTe QDs QDs (λemission=530 nm). The slight red-shifts were attributed to the formation of the hybrid structure, which could increase the effective size of the CdTe QDs and reduce the quantum size effect, resulting in a red shift of the photoluminescence maximum [54–56]. In addition, the fluorescence intensity of AuNP@MIPs-CdTe QDs was slightly lower than AuNP@NIPs-CdTe QDs, which could be attributed to the residue of template molecules during elution.

The fluorescent spectra of CdTe QDs and AuNP@MIPs-CdTe QDs with different MIPs thickness are provided in Fig. 3 B. The emission intensity of AuNP@MIPs-CdTe QDs with MIPs thickness of 8.5 ± 0.9 nm was 2.1-fold higher than that of fluorophore CdTe QDs (the residue of template molecules during elution was considered), which is attributed to the fact that the current in the metal can enhance the radiation dipole and emission through optical antenna effects [18]. When the MIPs thickness was 3.1 ± 0.4 nm, the fluorescence of AuNP@MIPs-CdTe QDs was almost quenched due to the Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) effect [49,57]. Therefore, the AuNP@MIPs-CdTe QDs with MIPs thickness of 8.5 ± 0.9 nm was selected for further study.

To gain a theoretical understanding of the fundamental principle of MEF system, FDTD calculations were utilized to simulate the distribution of EM field around AuNP, where the model was based on the size and structure of the TEM images. Fig. 3 C illustrates the simulated field in XYZ coordinates, and the incident light was polarized parallel to the underlying AuNP and aligned in the direction of the interparticle axis. The strong EM field enhancement around AuNP was observed. The maximum corresponding excitation rate enhancement, V (=|E/E 0|4), reached 6 at the position about 3–10 nm away from AuNP surface, while the value on the surface of AuNP was as low as 0.2, which is consistent with the literature [49].

To study the fluorescence quenching mechanism of AuNP@MIPs-CdTe QDs in the presence of OTC, the transient fluorescence lifetimes were investigated. The time-resolved fluorescence decay curves of AuNP@MIPs-CdTe QDs in the absence and presence of OTC were exhibited in Fig. 3 D, and they were fitted via the second-order fit as

, which could indicate that the quenching was a type of dynamic quenching. Dynamic quenching can be occurred by an energy-transfer or electron-transfer mechanism [58]. However, as the emission spectrum of AuNP@MIPs-CdTe QDs did not overlap with the absorption peak of OTC (Fig. S7), its fluorescence quenching mechanism could not be fluorescence resonance energy transfer. During the recognition process, CdTe QDs as an electron donor and OTC as an electron acceptor. As the UV absorption of OTC was very close to the conduction band of CdTe QDs [55], the excited electrons could directly transition to the LUMO UV and the visible energy level of UV. As a result, the excited electrons return to the ground state, causing the CdTe QDs to undergo fluorescence quenching (Fig. 3 E) [55]. Therefore, we propose that the quenching mechanism was electron transfer from AuNP@MIPs-CdTe QDs to OTC.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimize the experimental conditions of OTC to attain the optimal fluorescence response
ce:section.ce:para: The optimization of experimental parameters for AuNP@MIPs-CdTe QDs sensor was performed mainly including elution time, rebinding time, solution acidity, temperature, and the ratio of template molecular to functional monomer. The quenching rate, defined as F 0/F-1, was utilized as the index of the quenching efficiency, where F 0 and F were the fluorescent intensities of AuNP@MIPs-CdTe QDs sensor in the absence and presence of OTC, respectively [59].

The elution and rebinding performances of AuNP@MIPs-CdTe QDs sensor were investigated. As shown in Table S1 , the elution efficiency of different elution solutions was evaluated using varying volume ratios of ethanol, ammonia, and water. The results demonstrated that Vethanol/Vconcentrated ammonia/Vwater= 7:2:1 was the optimal elution solution. Fig. 4 A reveals that OTC could be completely removed after elution for 8 min, resulting in the restoration of fluorescence intensity of AuNP@MIPs-CdTe QDs sensor. When the sensor was eluted for 10 min, slight fluorescence quenching occurred, which was due to the re-adsorption of a small amount of OTC in the elution solution, and OTC was re-recognized by AuNP@MIPs-CdTe QDs sensor. Furthermore, long-term elution could cause damage to the imprinted site, resulting in affecting its selective detection performance.

The time-dependent evolution of signal intensities analysis in Fig. 4 B implies that the adsorption rate increased rapidly in the initial 6 min, and then reached adsorption equilibrium at 10 min. The reason for this phenomenon is probably that there were enough available active binding sites on MIPs at the initial stage, and the sites gradually decreased due to the occupation of OTC molecules. Compared with AuNP@NIPs-CdTe QDs sensor, AuNP@MIPs-CdTe QDs sensor exhibited a higher fluorescence quenching rate due to the specific recognition of imprinted holes, thereby the as-prepared AuNP@MIPs-CdTe QDs sensor processed the potential performance for selective recognition of OTC in real samples.

The influence of pH on the fluorescence detection properties of AuNP@MIPs-CdTe QDs sensor was examined by measuring the fluorescence intensity with and without OTC under different pH conditions ranging from 3 to 12. Fig. 4 C shows that the quenching rate of AuNP@MIPs-CdTe QDs sensor was affected by high acidity, likely due to the reduced strength of H-bonds in acidic solutions [60]. Similarly, high alkalinity could also significantly destroy the binding interactions between OTC and the imprinted sites. AuNP@MIPs-CdTe QDs sensor dispersed in PBS buffer solution with pH 5–11 exhibited excellent sensitivity and approached a maximum value at pH 7. Therefore, pH 7 was selected for the subsequent experiments to reduce experimental interference. Additionally, the effect of temperature ranging from 10 to 60 ℃ on the fluorescence quenching of AuNP@MIPs-CdTe QDs sensor was investigated (Fig. S8). The best quenching rate was achieved at around 25 ℃. Therefore, all the following experiments can be carried out at room temperature.

The influence on the sensitivity of detection exerted by the mole ratio of the MIPs precursor was investigated. Based on the results of MIP shell thickness control (see
Section 3.2 ), the TEOS concentration was set at an optimal value of 200 μL 20% TEOS in ethanol. The result in Fig. 4 D shows that AuNP@MIPs-CdTe QDs sensor presented excellent detection performance as the molar ratio of OTC to APTES 1:8. A low amount of APTES resulted in fewer functional groups available to bind with OTC, whereas excessive APTES caused self-condensation and reduced the formation of specific identification cavities, so the sensitivity of the sensor was relatively low. Thus, the AuNP@MIPs-CdTe QDs were prepared at a template-to-monomer ratio of 1:8.

ce:section.ce:section-title: MD simulation to investigate the nature of imprinting
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the imprinting process of the pre-polymerization mixture, MD simulations were conducted. Based on the optimal ratio of APTES, TEOS, and OTC obtained in the experiments, 10 OTC molecules, 80 APTES molecules, 200 TEOS molecules, and 2000 water molecules were added to the simulation box. Fig. 5 A represents molecules snapshots before and after polymerization by MD simulation. Initially, OTC molecules, APTES molecules, TEOS molecules, and water molecules were randomly distributed in the simulation box. After polymerization, the system acquired a totally different arrangement with OTC molecules concentrated at the right of the box and surrounded by APTES molecules. The molecular dynamics simulation process is provided in Video S1.
Supplementary material related to this article can be found online at doi:10.1016/j.jhazmat.2023.131941.

The following is the Supplementary material related to this article Videos S1
Video S1
.

To further explore the investigative MIPs recognition mechanism, the radial distribution functions (RDFs) were calculated to find out the interaction sites in the pre-polymerization system. The interactions between TEOS and OTC were not considered in the calculations due to the presence of inert functional groups (methyl) in TEOS molecules, which results in weaker interactions with OTC. Therefore, the possible interaction sites between functional groups (amino groups) of APTES and OTC were explored. The addressed sites (N, O1-O9) are labelled in Fig. 5 B. The interaction between NAPTES and O1OTC-O9OTC was studied and the peak of the curve represents the most possible distance between the two atoms. RDFs results in Fig. 5 C reveal that the possible binding sites of APTES and OTC were mainly from the H-bond formation. Specifically, through the RDFs calculation, it was found that the interactions between hydroxyl oxygen (O1OTC and O9OTC) or carbonyl oxygen (O2OTC) of OTC and NAPTES were not established because of the distance ||O-N||＞3.5 Å (See SI S3 for details on the formation principle of H-bond). In contrast, NAPTES-O3OTC, NAPTES-O4OTC, and NAPTES-O5OTC exhibited a narrow peak at approximately 3 Å, supporting the idea of the existence of H-bonds. Besides, since the positions of the O3OTC, O4OTC, and O5OTC were relatively close, there could be the most possible binding site. Although O6OTC and O7OTC were on the opposite side of O1-O5OTC, their interaction with NAPTES was also confirmed. Among them, the RDFs of NAPTES-O7OTC showed a wide peak at 2–6 Å, which could be explained by the repulsive force from the methyl group near O7OTC. The highest peak among the atoms represented the strong interaction between NAPTES and O8 OTC. Based on this analysis, it was inferred that O3-O8OTC could establish interactions with NAPTES. The binding sites of OTC and APTES were predicted to occur at NAPTES-O3/O4/O5OTC, NAPTES-O6/O7OTC, and NAPTES-O8OTC, respectively. In addition, the average number of H-bonds per frame were also calculated and presented in Table S2. The average number of H-bonds per frame provided by the binding sites of NAPTES-O3/O4/O5OTC, NAPTES-O6/O7OTC, and NAPTES-O8OTC were 0.63, 0.88, 0.48 respectively, which exhibited significantly higher than NAPTES-O1OTC (0.24), NAPTES-O2OTC (0.09), NAPTES-O9OTC (0.05). The results can also support the facts presented from RDFs analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantitative detection of OTC by AuNP@MIPs-CdTe QDs sensor
ce:section.ce:para:
After optimizing the experimental conditions, the performance of AuNP@MIPs-CdTe QDs sensor was evaluated for the quantitative determination of OTC. Both AuNP@MIPs-CdTe QDs and AuNP@NIPs-CdTe QDs sensors were used to detect OTC solutions in various concentrations (0–3.0 μM), and the fluorescence spectra of these solutions after shaking and incubating were recorded respectively. The fluorescence intensity of AuNP@MIPs-CdTe QDs sensor decreased gradually with an increase of OTC concentration in the detection system (
Fig. 6 A). This phenomenon can be attributed to the presence of numerous specific recognition sites on AuNP@MIPs-CdTe QDs sensor that could complement OTC after eluting the template molecules. Whereas, the fluorescence intensity of AuNP@NIPs-CdTe QDs was only slightly quenched ( Fig. 6 B and Fig. S9 ), possibly due to non-specific adsorption between them. As demonstrated in Figs. 6 C and 6 D, the MIPs and NIPs fluorescent sensors both displayed a well-defined linear fluorescence quenching for OTC: for AuNP@MIPs-CdTe QDs, it was fitted as F 0/F-1 = 0.805[C]+ 0.0286 with a coefficient of determination (R2) 0.998; for AuNP@NIPs-CdTe QDs sensor, it was F 0/F-1 = 0.132[C]+ 0.0137 with a coefficient of determination (R2) 0.985. The imprinting factor (IF) (Eq. 2), which was the standard for evaluating MIP-based fluorescent sensors, was evaluated by comparing the
K
SV
of MIPs and NIPs fluorescent sensors [61],
(2)

K
SV ,
MIPs
K
SV ,

where
K
SV , MIPs
and
K
SV , NIPs
denote the linear slopes of the fitted equations, respectively. The IF of AuNP@MIPs-CdTe QDs sensor for detecting OTC was calculated to be 6.10, indicating the effective imprinting and specific recognition ability for OTC. In addition, AuNP@MIPs-CdTe QDs sensor exhibited a calculated limit of detection (LOD) of 5.22 nM for OTC detection, which was determined by the equation, LOD = 3σ/S, where σ represents the standard deviation of the blank signal, and S represents the slope of the calibration curve).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity, reproducibility, stability, and reversibility of AuNP@MIPs-CdTe QDs sensor for the detection of OTC
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the selectivity of AuNP@MIPs-CdTe QDs sensor for OTC detection, TC, doxycycline (DOX), enrofloxacin (ENR), and florfenicol (FF) with a similar molecular structure to OTC were selected as analogs and studied under the same conditions. The selectivity measurements with the details were provided in SI S4. As shown in Fig. 7 A, although AuNP@NIPs-CdTe QDs sensor had a certain response to OTC and its structural analogs, its quenching efficiency was rather low and unable to recognize a specific target. In contrast, the relative quenching efficiency of AuNP@MIPs-CdTe QDs for OTC was much higher than those for analogs, which was attributed to the MIPs layer specific recognition for OTC. Moreover, when AuNP@MIPs-CdTe QDs sensor was incubated in OTC solution, mixed with a 2-fold, 5-fold, and 10-fold excess of TC, DOX, ENR, and FF for competitive binding, the results (Fig. S10) show that the fluorescence quenching rate of AuNP@MIPs-CdTe QDs sensor only slightly decreased compared to that in the bare OTC solution, and AuNP@NIPs-CdTe QDs sensor displayed lower anti-interference ability than AuNP@MIPs-CdTe QDs sensor. To further investigate the selectivity performance of AuNP@MIPs-CdTe QDs sensor in the actual environmental detection applications, the potential influence of common ions (Na+, K+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Cl-, I-, CO3 2-, and NO3 -) was analyzed. As depicted in Fig. S11 and Fig. S12, AuNP@MIPs-CdTe QDs sensor almost displayed no effect on the detection of OTC in the presence of ion interferences. Thereby, all results demonstrated that AuNP@MIPs-CdTe QDs sensor had high selectivity for detecting OTC.

The reproducibility of AuNP@MIPs-CdTe QDs sensor was studied by measuring 40 different samples of OTC with the same concentration of 1 μM with a relative standard deviation (RSD) of 1.9% (Fig. 7 B). The photochemical stability of AuNP@MIPs-CdTe QDs sensor was investigated by monitoring changes in fluorescence intensity against irradiation time under 365 nm UV lamp irradiation. As shown in Fig. S13, the fluorescence intensity of the sensor remained relatively stable without significant differences (<0.8%) observed during a 60-min irradiation period, indicating that its excellent photobleaching resistance. Furthermore, the storage stability of AuNP@MIPs-CdTe QDs sensor was evaluated by the repeated detection of OTC within 10 days under the same detection conditions, and it retained 92.5% of its initial fluorescence quenching rate (Fig. S14). These results demonstrate the excellent reproducibility and stability of the AuNP@MIPs-CdTe QDs sensor.

Since the continuous elution process could cause damage to the identification chamber, it is necessary to study the reversibility of AuNP@MIPs-CdTe QDs sensor under removal-rebinding recycling. The fluorescence intensity of the sensor before and after the adsorption of OTC at a concentration of 1 μM remained at about 92.4% of the original fluorescence intensity after five cycles (Fig. S15 and Table S3). The excellent detection efficiency of AuNP@MIPs-CdTe QDs sensor, even after multiple cycles, could be attributed to the fact that H-bonded MIPs were more easily damaged by the eluent compared to covalently bonded MIPs, resulted in enhanced imprinting efficiency. The slight reduction in the detection efficiency was due to the destruction of MIPs cavities during the regeneration process. This result indicated that AuNP@MIPs-CdTe QDs sensor maintained its excellent detection and recovery efficiency, which was significantly superior over disposable materials.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of OTC in real water sample analysis
ce:section.ce:para: The practicability of AuNP@MIPs-CdTe QDs sensor was evaluated by detecting different concentrations of OTC in real water samples including tap water, lake water, and river water. The average recovery and its RSD for triplicate measurements at each concentration were obtained vis the standard recovery method. The results summarized in Table 1 demonstrate satisfactory recoveries in the range of 96.0–103.0% with RSDs of 1.4–4.6% for water samples. Therefore, these results demonstrate that AuNP@MIPs-CdTe QDs sensor for practical applications was capable of accurately detecting trace OTC in complex aqueous environments.

Table 2 presents the performance comparison of AuNP@MIPs-CdTe QDs sensor with previously reported sensors for detecting OTC [62–68,38]. As observed in the table, the recognition systems were based on “turn-on”, “turn-off” and ratiometric fluorescence. The traditional fluorescence sensor demonstrated high sensitivity but low selectivity for directly detecting OTC [69]. Recently, SMI-Fl sensors have been developed to further improve selectivity. Most of support materials of SMI-Fl sensors such as QDs [38], MOFs [62], and SiO2 [66] have a large specific surface area to improve the imprinting efficiency of MIPs. In this work, AuNP@MIPs-CdTe QDs sensor for detecting OTC exhibited lower LOD compared to those reported previously, and the IF of 6.10 was significantly higher than that of others, demonstrating the exceptional sensitivity and selectivity of the developed AuNP@MIPs-CdTe QDs sensor.

In summary, the reported MIPs-isolated AuNP-enhanced fluorescence sensor, AuNP@MIPs-CdTe QDs, was successfully developed for detecting trace OTC in water samples. By combining the strong fluorescence signal of MEF effect, high selectivity of SMIPs, and the stability of CdTe QDs, the developed AuNP@MIPs-CdTe QDs sensor exhibited excellent specific selective recognition and highly sensitive detection for OTC. The maximum fluorescence enhancement was obtained with a MIPs shell of 8.5 ± 0.9 nm, and the sensor had a low LOD of 5.22 nM (2.40 μg/L), which was slightly lower than those reported, and the IF of 6.10 was much higher than the others. The reproducibility, stability, and reversibility of AuNP@MIPs-CdTe QDs sensor were all proved satisfactory. With more virtues of an excellent environment-friendly fabrication route and handy convenience, the MIPs-isolated metal-enhanced fluorescence sensor shows great promise for on-site application in environmental monitoring fields. Our future efforts will focus on optimizing SMIPs-based fluorescence sensing materials to enhance adsorption kinetics and capacity, as well as investigating more theoretical support for the synergistic fluorescence enhancement mechanism between SMIPs and metallic nanostructures.

Oxytetracycline is a hazardous material which excreted in its original form via feces and urine after ingestion and absorption owing to its incomplete degradation, leading to the long-term accumulation in water environment. This not only has adverse impacts on the whole ecosystem but also poses harm to human health through transmission in the food chain. To detect trace oxytetracycline in complex matrices, MIPs-isolated AuNP-enhanced fluorescence sensor in which specific recognition MIPs served as an isolation layer to adjust the distance between AuNP and CdTe QDs to optimize MEF system, provides a promising approach in the water environment.

Yuanyuan Yang: Methodology, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Xiaohui Liu: Investigation on part of Molecular Dynamics, Writing – review & editing. Shuang Meng: Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Shun Mao: Writing – review & editing. Wenquan Tao: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Zhuo Li: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition, Supervision.
